items.prompt_md: Why does XOR-ing every element of the array together isolate the single non-duplicated value?
options: Because XOR is only defined for two operands, so pairs always cancel
Because `x ^ x = 0` and `x ^ 0 = x`, so every duplicate pair cancels to zero and only the lone value survives
Because XOR sorts values in ascending order as it combines them
Because XOR is equivalent to summing the array modulo 2
items.answer: Because `x ^ x = 0` and `x ^ 0 = x`, so every duplicate pair cancels to zero and only the lone value survives
items.explanation_md: XOR is commutative and associative, so regardless of order every value that appears twice cancels itself out (`x ^ x = 0`), leaving only the unpaired value XORed with 0 — itself.

items.prompt_md: Every value in the array appears exactly twice except one value, which appears once. You need to find it in O(n) time and O(1) extra space. Which approach fits?
options: Sort the array, then scan for the position where neighbors differ
Keep a hash map counting occurrences of each value
XOR every element together — duplicate pairs cancel to zero, leaving the answer
Use two pointers converging from both ends of the array
items.answer: XOR every element together — duplicate pairs cancel to zero, leaving the answer
items.explanation_md: The O(1)-space requirement rules out the hash map, and sorting/two-pointers don't exploit the pairing structure at all. A single XOR accumulator cancels every duplicate in one pass with no extra memory.

items.prompt_md: What is the time and space complexity of the one-pass XOR solution to find the single number?
options: O(n log n) time, O(1) extra space
items.explanation_md: One pass over the array, one XOR per element, and a single integer accumulator — O(n) time and O(1) extra space, no map or sort required.
